seo.title: Cybersecurity RAG Pipeline AI Agent Checklist
seo.description: Production readiness checklist for rag pipeline AI agents in cybersecurity. Covers NIST Cybersecurity Framework, SOC 2 Type II, ISO 27001.

content.intro: Deploying a RAG pipeline in cybersecurity environments introduces unique risks where AI-synthesized threat intelligence can be weaponized if leaked, misattributed, or hallucinated. This checklist ensures your retrieval-augmented system meets NIST CSF, SOC 2 Type II, and ISO 27001 requirements while preventing adversary exploitation of AI-generated outputs. Every item is scoped to the specific failure modes of vector search, context assembly, and LLM synthesis in high-stakes security operations.

sections.heading: Data Ingestion & Vector Store Security
sections.description: Controls to ensure threat intelligence documents are securely ingested, classified, and stored in the vector database without exposing sensitive indicators to unauthorized retrieval paths.

items.label: Classify all ingested documents by sensitivity tier before embedding
items.description: Tag each document (e.g., TLP:RED, TLP:AMBER, TLP:WHITE) prior to vectorization so retrieval filters can enforce access controls at query time. Use automated classification tools aligned with CISA TLP guidelines.

items.label: Encrypt vector embeddings at rest using AES-256
items.description: Apply field-level encryption to all stored embeddings in the vector database. Ensure encryption keys are managed via a dedicated KMS (e.g., AWS KMS, HashiCorp Vault) with rotation policies meeting ISO 27001 A.10.1 requirements.

items.label: Implement namespace isolation per classification level in the vector store
items.description: Partition vector collections by data sensitivity tier so that a query scoped to TLP:WHITE cannot retrieve TLP:RED embeddings. Validate namespace boundaries with automated penetration tests quarterly.

items.label: Audit all document ingestion events with immutable logging
items.description: Log every document ingestion event including source URL, hash, classification tag, and ingesting user to a WORM-compliant log store. This satisfies SOC 2 Type II CC7.2 and ISO 27001 A.12.4 audit trail requirements.

items.label: Validate document provenance before embedding via hash verification
items.description: Compute and store SHA-256 hashes of all source documents at ingestion time. Re-verify hashes before each retrieval cycle to detect tampering or supply chain poisoning of the threat intelligence corpus.

items.label: Restrict vector store write access to authenticated ingestion pipelines only
items.description: Enforce service account-based authentication with least-privilege IAM roles for any process that writes to the vector store. Disable direct human write access in production and enforce MFA for administrative access.

items.label: Scan ingested threat intelligence documents for embedded malicious payloads
items.description: Run all PDFs, HTML exports, and structured feeds through a sandboxed malware scanner before ingestion. Prevent adversary-crafted documents from injecting prompt injection payloads into the RAG context window.

sections.heading: Retrieval Pipeline & Context Assembly Controls
sections.description: Safeguards applied during vector search and context window construction to prevent unauthorized data exposure, context poisoning, and retrieval of adversary-accessible intelligence.

items.label: Enforce user-scoped retrieval filters based on authenticated identity claims
items.description: Pass the authenticated user's clearance level and role as metadata filters on every vector search query. Ensure the retrieval layer rejects queries that attempt to override or bypass these filters, validated by unit tests on every deployment.

items.label: Limit retrieved context window to minimum necessary chunks (need-to-know principle)
items.description: Cap the number of retrieved document chunks per query to the minimum required for the task (e.g., top-3 to top-5). Avoid assembling context windows that aggregate classified indicators from multiple sources unnecessarily.

items.label: Strip PII and classified IOCs from context before sending to external LLM APIs
items.description: Apply a pre-LLM redaction layer that detects and masks IP addresses, CVE IDs marked TLP:RED, actor TTPs, and victim organization names before the assembled context is transmitted to any third-party LLM API endpoint.

items.label: Log all retrieval queries and assembled contexts with user attribution
items.description: Record every retrieval query, the top-k results returned, and the final assembled context to an immutable audit log. Include user ID, timestamp, and session token to support SOC 2 Type II CC6.1 access monitoring.

items.label: Implement context relevance scoring threshold to reject low-confidence retrievals
items.description: Set a minimum cosine similarity threshold (e.g., ≥0.75) below which retrieved chunks are discarded rather than included in the context. This prevents tangentially related but sensitive documents from leaking into responses.

items.label: Detect and block prompt injection attempts in user queries before retrieval
items.description: Apply an input validation layer that scans user queries for known prompt injection patterns (e.g., 'ignore previous instructions', role-override attempts) before executing vector search. Log and alert on all detected attempts.

items.label: Validate that assembled context does not exceed data residency boundaries
items.description: For regulated environments, verify that retrieved document chunks originate only from data stores within approved geographic regions before assembly. Enforce this check programmatically using document metadata tags.

sections.heading: LLM Output Verification & Hallucination Controls
sections.description: Processes to verify that AI-synthesized security advisories are grounded in retrieved sources, accurately cite CVE databases, and do not fabricate threat intelligence that could trigger false incident responses.

items.label: Implement automated source faithfulness scoring on every LLM response
items.description: Use a secondary verification model or NLI-based faithfulness scorer (e.g., TRUE, SummaC) to confirm that every factual claim in the LLM output is supported by a retrieved source chunk. Flag responses with faithfulness score below 0.85 for human review.

items.label: Cross-validate all CVE references in AI outputs against the NVD API in real time
items.description: Before delivering any AI-generated advisory to analysts, programmatically query the NIST National Vulnerability Database API to verify that each cited CVE ID exists, matches the described severity, and has not been rejected or disputed.

items.label: Require inline source citations with document ID and chunk reference for every claim
items.description: Configure the LLM prompt to mandate that each factual statement includes a bracketed citation referencing the specific retrieved document ID and chunk index. Validate citation presence programmatically before response delivery.

items.label: Flag and quarantine responses containing threat actor TTPs not present in retrieved context
items.description: Run a post-generation check that extracts all MITRE ATT&CK technique references from the LLM output and verifies each one appears in the retrieved context. Quarantine and escalate any response referencing techniques absent from the source documents.

items.label: Apply hallucination detection for numeric claims (CVSS scores, affected versions, dates)
items.description: Extract all numeric values from LLM outputs (CVSS scores, version numbers, disclosure dates) and verify each against the retrieved source chunks using exact-match validation. Reject responses with unverifiable numeric claims.

items.label: Enforce human-in-the-loop review for all AI-generated advisories before distribution
items.description: Route every AI-synthesized security advisory through a mandatory analyst review queue before it is shared with stakeholders or triggers any downstream workflow. Document reviewer identity and approval timestamp for SOC 2 audit evidence.

items.label: Maintain a response audit trail linking each output to its exact retrieved context snapshot
items.description: Store a cryptographically signed snapshot of the exact context window used to generate each response alongside the output. This enables post-incident forensic analysis to determine whether a false advisory was caused by retrieval failure or LLM hallucination.

items.label: Set confidence thresholds below which the system must abstain rather than generate
items.description: Define explicit abstention conditions: if retrieval returns fewer than 2 relevant chunks above the similarity threshold, the system must respond with 'insufficient evidence' rather than synthesizing a response from low-confidence context.

sections.heading: Automated Response Governance & Incident Controls
sections.description: Approval workflows, kill switches, and audit mechanisms to ensure AI-recommended or AI-triggered response actions cannot escalate incidents, cause outages, or execute on critical systems without verified human authorization.

items.label: Classify all potential response actions by criticality tier before automation eligibility
items.description: Define a tiered action taxonomy: Tier 1 (log enrichment, alert tagging) may be automated; Tier 2 (firewall rule changes, account lockouts) require single analyst approval; Tier 3 (system isolation, service shutdown) require dual-control authorization from senior security staff.

items.label: Implement a mandatory human approval gate for all Tier 2 and Tier 3 automated actions
items.description: Integrate the RAG pipeline's action recommendations with your SOAR platform's approval workflow. No Tier 2 or Tier 3 action may execute without a time-stamped, identity-verified approval from an authorized analyst, logged to the immutable audit trail.

items.label: Deploy a hardware-enforced kill switch to halt all automated AI response actions within 60 seconds
items.description: Implement a kill switch accessible to SOC leadership that immediately suspends all pending and future automated actions from the AI pipeline. Test the kill switch monthly and document response time in the incident response runbook.

items.label: Enforce a dry-run mode for all new automated response playbooks before production activation
items.description: Require that any new AI-generated response playbook runs in simulation mode for a minimum of 5 business days, logging intended actions without executing them. Conduct a formal review of simulation logs before promoting to production.

items.label: Monitor automated action false positive rates and trigger circuit breakers at defined thresholds
items.description: Track the false positive rate of AI-recommended actions per playbook. If a playbook's false positive rate exceeds 5% in a 24-hour window, automatically suspend that playbook and page the on-call security engineer for review.

items.label: Require rollback procedures for every automated action before deployment authorization
items.description: For each automated response action, document and test a rollback procedure (e.g., firewall rule reversion, account re-enablement script) before the action is approved for production. Store rollback scripts in version control with access controls.

items.label: Log all AI action recommendations with full context lineage for post-incident review
items.description: For every action recommended by the RAG pipeline, store the triggering alert, retrieved context, LLM reasoning chain, recommended action, approval status, and execution result in a linked audit record. Retain for minimum 12 months per SOC 2 requirements.

items.label: Conduct quarterly red team exercises specifically targeting AI pipeline manipulation and action escalation
items.description: Engage an internal or third-party red team to attempt adversarial manipulation of the RAG pipeline (prompt injection, context poisoning, approval bypass) to trigger unauthorized automated actions. Document findings and remediate within 30 days.

notes.framework: NIST Cybersecurity Framework (CSF 2.0) — Govern & Protect Functions
notes.note: Under CSF 2.0's new Govern function, AI systems used in cybersecurity operations must be included in the organization's cybersecurity risk management strategy. Specifically, the RAG pipeline's data flows, retrieval access controls, and automated action capabilities must be documented in the organizational risk register. The Protect function (PR.DS) requires that threat intelligence data processed by the RAG pipeline be protected against unauthorized disclosure, directly mandating the TLP-based namespace isolation and pre-LLM redaction controls in this checklist.

notes.framework: SOC 2 Type II — CC6 (Logical and Physical Access) & CC7 (System Operations)
notes.note: SOC 2 Type II auditors will scrutinize access controls on the vector store (CC6.1, CC6.3) and the completeness of system monitoring logs (CC7.2). The RAG pipeline must demonstrate that retrieval queries are user-scoped, that all access to classified embeddings is logged with user attribution, and that anomalous retrieval patterns (e.g., bulk extraction of threat intelligence) trigger automated alerts. Evidence of these controls must be continuously collected and available for the 12-month audit period.

notes.framework: ISO 27001:2022 — A.8.24 (Use of Cryptography) & A.5.23 (Information Security for Cloud Services)
notes.note: ISO 27001:2022 Annex A.8.24 requires a formal cryptography policy covering encryption of data at rest and in transit, directly applicable to vector embedding encryption and API communications with LLM providers. A.5.23 mandates that cloud service agreements (including LLM API providers) include security requirements aligned with the organization's information security policies. Organizations must review LLM provider data processing agreements to confirm that threat intelligence submitted in API calls is not retained for model training.

notes.framework: CISA Directives — BOD 23-01 & AI Cybersecurity Guidance
notes.note: CISA's Binding Operational Directive 23-01 requires continuous asset visibility and vulnerability enumeration, which AI-generated advisories must support rather than contradict. CISA's 2024 guidance on AI in cybersecurity explicitly warns against over-reliance on AI for critical infrastructure response decisions, reinforcing the human-in-the-loop requirements for Tier 2 and Tier 3 actions. Federal agencies and critical infrastructure operators must additionally ensure that any LLM API used in the RAG pipeline is not hosted by a vendor subject to foreign adversary data access laws.

cta.heading: Automate Your Cybersecurity Agent Compliance
cta.description: Use Vex to continuously monitor your RAG pipeline against this checklist, automatically flag compliance gaps in your vector store access controls and LLM output verification, and generate audit-ready evidence for SOC 2 Type II and ISO 27001 assessments.
